narrative_blocks.header: Twin children born 1869
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue and Sarah Chipman had twins — Ralph Josiah and Anna Eliza — born together September 24, 1869. Anna died June 27, 1884 at about fifteen; Ralph survived. A third child, Robert Shields, was also born on September 24 — seven years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son John Hooker died at twenty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah's son John Hooker Goodhue — named for the family lineage — died August 20, 1883 at Whitewater at twenty-one years old. The name Hooker had been carried through three generations of this branch and continued in the children who survived.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to eighty-nine in Whitewater; wife also eighty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue died August 10, 1919 at eighty-nine years old in Wisconsin, having outlived his father Rev. Josiah Fletcher Goodhue by fifty-six years. His wife Sarah Bennett Chipman Goodhue also reached eighty-eight — both achieving remarkable longevity for the Wisconsin farming community they had settled.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin children born 1869
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue and Sarah Chipman had twins — Ralph Josiah and Anna Eliza — born together September 24, 1869. Anna died June 27, 1884 at about fifteen; Ralph survived. A third child, Robert Shields, was also born on September 24 — seven years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son John Hooker died at twenty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah's son John Hooker Goodhue — named for the family lineage — died August 20, 1883 at Whitewater at twenty-one years old. The name Hooker had been carried through three generations of this branch and continued in the children who survived.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to eighty-nine in Whitewater; wife also eighty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue died August 10, 1919 at eighty-nine years old in Wisconsin, having outlived his father Rev. Josiah Fletcher Goodhue by fifty-six years. His wife Sarah Bennett Chipman Goodhue also reached eighty-eight — both achieving remarkable longevity for the Wisconsin farming community they had settled.

narrative_blocks.header: Married in Omaha 1882; settled at Holton, Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: George Harris Goodhue married Nellie Rockwood on February 9, 1882 at Omaha, Nebraska — a frontier railroad hub — and the family settled at Holton, Michigan. He was the eldest surviving child of Josiah and Sarah Chipman Goodhue of Whitewater, Wisconsin.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in Kalamazoo 1944
narrative_blocks.body: George Harris Goodhue died April 25, 1944 in Oshtemo, Kalamazoo County, Michigan. His wife Mary Ella 'Nellie' Rockwood Goodhue died July 1, 1944 in Kalamazoo — the couple died in the same Michigan county within two months of each other at ages 84 and 85.

narrative_blocks.header: Died two months after her husband, Kalamazoo 1944
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Ella 'Nellie' Rockwood Goodhue died July 1, 1944 in Kalamazoo, Michigan — two months after her husband George Harris Goodhue died in the same county on April 25. Both were buried in Hillside Cemetery back in Whitewater, Wisconsin.

narrative_blocks.header: Became Dr. Lolita Goodhue; lived to ninety-six
narrative_blocks.body: Lolita Goodhue — daughter of George Harris Goodhue and Nellie Rockwood — became a physician, known as Dr. Lolita Goodhue, a remarkable achievement for a woman born in 1883 in Lima, Wisconsin. She lived to about ninety-six, dying in 1979.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Rockwood after his mother; married 1908
narrative_blocks.body: Edmund Rockwood Goodhue carried his mother Nellie Rockwood's surname as his middle name — a naming tribute to the maternal line. He married Amanda Diechoff on November 28, 1908.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Rockwood honors mother Nellie Rockwood Goodhue's maiden name.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived the century: born 1896, died 1998
narrative_blocks.body: Katherine Doris 'Katie' Goodhue MacDonell was born in 1896 and died in 1998, living past one hundred years and spanning the entire twentieth century from start to end. Her grandmother Elizabeth White Hooker Goodhue had also been long-lived, dying at eighty-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 1904; lived to see the year 2000
narrative_blocks.body: Frances E. Goodhue Loder was born September 18, 1904 in White Cloud, Michigan and lived until 2000 — ninety-five or ninety-six years old, and the last of the George Harris Goodhue branch to reach the new millennium. Her sister Katherine Doris had lived to 1998, making two centenarian-adjacent sisters.

narrative_blocks.header: Died as a toddler in 1863
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker Goodhue was born December 21, 1861 and died in 1863 at about two years old — a toddler's death, not the young adult's death suggested by the Edward Hopkins genealogy, which recorded his death year as '1833' (an impossible date, corrected to 1863 by FindAGrave). He was the first of two Goodhue children named to carry the Hooker family lineage.

naming_inspiration.description: Named John Hooker — honoring the Hooker lineage through his great-grandmother Elizabeth White Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter married Edgar Davis
narrative_blocks.body: Helen Frances Goodhue married Edgar Davis on December 1, 1887, and the family settled in Richmond, Wisconsin. Their son Robert Goodhue Davis died September 9, 1899 at about eight years old — a loss at the threshold of childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eight years old in Richmond, Wisconsin
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Goodhue Davis was born in September 1891 at Richmond, Wisconsin, and died September 9, 1899 at about eight years old. He was the first child of Edgar Davis and Helen Frances Goodhue.

career.notes: Controlled manufacturing of radar tubes for Salt Lake City defenses in WWII. Per obituary/family records.
Cost accountant. Controller at Gallagher Mining and Manufacturing.

narrative_blocks.header: WWII radar tube manufacturer; from Wisconsin to Utah
narrative_blocks.body: Kenneth Monroe Davis worked for GE in Chicago and ran a family trucking business in Wisconsin before moving west — during World War II he controlled the manufacturing of radar tubes for the defense of Salt Lake City. He settled in Salt Lake City as controller of Gallagher Mining and Manufacturing Company and lived to ninety, dying May 15, 1996.

narrative_blocks.header: Born the same day as twin Anna
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph Josiah and his twin Anna Eliza Goodhue were born together September 24, 1869 at Whitewater, Wisconsin. Anna died June 27, 1884 at about fifteen; Ralph survived. Three years before the twins, brother John Hooker had also died at twenty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin of Ralph
narrative_blocks.body: Anna Eliza Goodhue was born September 24, 1869 alongside her twin Ralph Josiah — and died June 27, 1884, at about fifteen, while her twin lived on. Brother John Hooker had also died young three years earlier; the family lost two children before forty in the Wisconsin years.

narrative_blocks.header: Born September 24
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Shields Goodhue was born September 24, 1876 — the same calendar date as his older twin siblings Ralph and Anna, born September 24, 1869. Whether coincidence or family tradition, three Goodhue children share the same birth date across a seven-year span.

narrative_blocks.header: Middlebury 1821, Andover Seminary
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Goodhue graduated Middlebury College in 1821 and Andover Theological Seminary thereafter, serving as Congregational pastor at Williston and Shoreham Vermont for twenty-four years. In 1857 he and Elizabeth removed to Whitewater Wisconsin, where he died May 3, 1863.

narrative_blocks.header: Published history of Shoreham, Vermont, 1861
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Goodhue published 'History of the Town of Shoreham, Vermont, from the date of its charter, October 8th, 1761, to the present time' in 1861, printed by A.H. Copeland of Middlebury — a local history completed while he was still pastor at Shoreham. His middle name Fletcher derived from his mother's family: his mother was Elisabeth Fletcher Goodhue.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Congregational minister; moved Vermont to Wisconsin
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth White Hooker married Rev. Josiah F. Goodhue in 1824 — a Middlebury College and Andover Theological Seminary graduate who served Congregational churches at Williston and Shoreham, Vermont, for twenty-four years. In 1857 they removed to Whitewater, Wisconsin, where he died in 1863 and she in January 1889 at eighty-eight.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #530: 'an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vt.'

narrative_blocks.header: Apothecary and grocer at Rutland; wife predeceased him
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker was an apothecary and grocer at Rutland, Vermont, where he spent his adult life and died in 1836 at sixty-five. His wife Sarah Brown died four years before him in 1832, predeceasing him despite being nine years younger.

narrative_blocks.header: Married daughter of Col. John Brown of Pittsfield
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield, Massachusetts, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. If confirmed, Thomas Hooker married the daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons: one to Boston missions
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington and Henry Brown Hooker were born the same day — August 31, 1802 — and their lives diverged completely: John moved to Chicago in 1836 among the city's earliest New England settlers, while Henry graduated Middlebury College, entered the ministry, and became Secretary of Missions at Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of five children died in Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: John Worthington Hooker moved to Chicago in 1836 and died there in 1866; his sister Julia Ann married a Rutland lawyer and also died in Chicago in 1842. Two of Thomas Hooker's five children ended their days in the city that barely existed when they were born.

narrative_blocks.header: Probably daughter of a Revolutionary War martyr
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Brown's father, Colonel John Brown of Pittsfield Massachusetts named in the Hooker genealogy, is almost certainly the Revolutionary War officer killed at the Battle of Stone Arabia, New York, on October 19, 1780 — when Sarah was barely a year old. She married Thomas Hooker in 1796 and died at Rutland, Vermont, in 1832, four years before her husband.

burialCemetery.name: Hillside Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Whitewater, Walworth County WI. FGA #88154. Contains Thomas Hooker Goodhue Sr., Josiah Goodhue, and many Goodhue-branch descendants.
